summary: Stancil uses the Jordan Chiles medal dispute to show how the era of rigorous quantification produced elaborate rules and data systems yet still failed to feel fair or objective. He argues that the cultural pendulum has swung from “data-driven” decision-making toward taste, intuition, and AI-mediated “vibes,” and that this shift is self-reinforcing as tools and organizations adapt to what people find most accessible.
money_quotes: In data we trusted; now, God is in the vibes.
If AI is good at anything, it is good at interpreting the vibes.
As, uh, Stalin once said, “quantity has a quality all its own.”
A generation grew up reading The Signal and the Noise and Thinking Fast and Slow, and bludgeoned the prior generation to death with models and quantitative rhetoric and complex codes of points.
key_insight: The culture that once prized quantified rigor is yielding to taste and AI-mediated intuition because “vibes” are faster, more compelling, and increasingly easy to operationalize.
